[00:00–00:30]
I call this regular meeting of the Spring Branch Independent School District Board of Trustees to
order. Let the record show the time is now six o'clock and a quorum of board members is present.
This meeting has been duly called, and the notice for this meeting has been posted in accordance
with the Texas Open Meetings Act, Texas Government Code, Chapter 551.041. Please stand while Trustee
Chris Ernest leads us in the pledges of allegiance to the U.S. and Texas flag.

[00:30–01:00]
I pledge allegiance to the flag and the United States of America and to the Republic for which it
stands one nation under God indivisible with liberty and justice for all. Under the Texas flag, I
pledge allegiance to the Texas state, under God, one and indivisible. I would like to introduce the
senior administrative staff. They are Ms. Linda Buckman, Associate Superintendent for Community.

[01:00–01:30]
Superintendent for Communications and Community Engagement, Ms. Karen Heath, Associate
Superintendent for Administration and Talent, Ms. Christina Massick, Associate Superintendent for
Information Technology, Dr. Annie Wolf, Associate Superintendent for Academics, Ms. Christine
Porter, Associate Superintendent for Finance, Mr. Travis Stanford, Associate Superintendent for
Operations, Dr. Jennifer Parker, Community Superintendent, Dr. Brian Williams, Community
Superintendent. I would like to introduce your board members from left to right. They are board
members.

[01:30–02:00]
They are board member David Slattery, Board Member Chris Ernest, Stacey Kim, Executive Assistant to
the Board, Dr. Jennifer Blaine, Superintendent, I am Lisa Alpey, Board President, Board Vice
President, Courtney Anderson, Board Secretary, Shannon Mahan, Board Member John Perez, and Board

acknowledge at the outset the tragic death of our

[02:00–02:30]
beloved SBISD police dispatcher Jose Torres. As many of you know, last Friday he was killed. His
death has left our entire community pretty heartbroken. He was beloved. He was taken by a senseless
act of violence as he worked to improve his new home for his wife and two beautiful daughters. His
untimely passing is a devastating loss not only to his family and friends, but to the Spring Branch
community and everyone who knew him. His dedication and service to our

[02:30–03:00]
police department and to the school district are felt by many, and he will be remembered for his
hard work, kindness, and the positive impact he made on all of our lives. We are grieving, but
please know that we want to do everything we can to support his family, and so we will be putting
out information on how anyone who wishes to support them can do so. Dr. Blaine, do you have any
opening remarks? Yes, I do, and thank you for that, President Alpe. Um so I do have several opening.

[03:00–03:30]
remarks. It's a bit lengthy, and I apologize. I don't usually speak this long, but I have several
things I wanted to share with everybody. The first thing is just really at this time of year, you
know, we're upon Thanksgiving break, and I just really want to start with gratitude. And it's just
really gratitude for our school district and all the members of our Spring Branch family, for our
employees, our parents, our students, our partners, our volunteers, our board of trustees who are
supportive of us. And it really takes everybody working together to have the outcomes that we have
right now, and we know we need to continue pushing forward and do.

[03:30–04:00]
pushing forward and doing great things, but certainly those things don't happen without the support
of the entire community. So thank you very much. And then I just want to, you know, point out that
it it really this collective greatness is everybody. You know, um we think about our academic staff
and they're they're tremendous, they're wonderful. But really, you know, we talk about our bus

our kids ride the bus and they're the first smiling face that our kids see and sometimes the last
one they might see when they get off the bus. So thank you to our bus drivers, our student nutrition
staff,

[04:30–05:00]
Staff that feed our kids, make sure that they have healthy meals, breakfast and lunch, our
custodians who clean our buildings and make sure that they are spotless and clean so that our kids
and our teachers are able to learn and teach in a healthy environment. Our coaches, our counselors,
our principals, our campus staff members, our all of our senior staff members, our HR team who make
sure that our classrooms are staffed. I mean, I could go on and on about everybody's area, but I
just wanted to tell you how grateful I am to be in the system and work.

[05:00–05:30]
Um with such amazing, amazing people. And I say all that because we sent a message out this morning
that really affirms the great work of our system. And that's really our academic results. And I want
to take a minute to highlight those. But again, I want to say that you know, it's our teachers, our
amazing teachers in the classroom, they are doing such an incredible job. Our principals on the
campus overseeing that instructional leadership, our academic staff, all of those people are so
important in making this happen for kids. But we don't want to not notice all the support people in
other and other

[05:30–06:00]
divisions that really are the backbone of how we get this work done. So wanted to share that there's
a local education nonprofit called Goodreason Houston in the Houston area. We work closely with
Goodreason Houston and have for several years. They are a wonderful partner to many, many school
districts, and they provide much needed funding and partnership and thought processes for all of uh
local school districts and charter programs. Um but they um have just recently um published a
dashboard on their website. It's a third party independent source.

[06:00–06:30]
And they are tracking, as I think we've talked about before, academic progress in the Houston area.
And Good Reason Houston's ultimate goal is that they want every child to have a robust academic
experience so that they are ready when they walk across the stage and that the sky's the limit and
they have those opportunities and they're post secondary ready. And all of their work really focuses
in that arena. And so they've published a dashboard recently that we learned about that tracks the
academic progress that has been made by all school districts in the Houston area. And if you go to
the dashboard, you'll see kindergarten readiness.

[06:30–07:00]
And readiness, you'll see third grade, you'll see fourth, fifth, sixth, you'll see third grade,
eighth grade readiness, and you'll see college readiness. All up there, and it's a drop-down. If you
go to the menu and you click, it will give you a listing of all the Houston area school districts
and charter programs that are part of their network, which is anybody in the Houston area, and they
have ranked the results. And I'm really proud to announce that Spring Branch ISD takes the top spot
in almost every single category. That's in kindergarten readiness, third grade reading, eighth grade
reading, college.

[07:00–07:30]
readiness, including SAT and ACT results. And I bring that about because I want to celebrate the
great work of this system, but there's another reason I wanted to say this in my opening comments,
and that's that as we think about this legislative session, and we are we know that we're going to
probably have vouchers. Our governor has said we're going to have vouchers, and he's determined to
bring that program to the state of Texas. And so our data affirm that we're doing great work for
kids. And when you go to the third grade list and you see Spring Branch sitting at the top at the
most critical indicator that a

[07:30–08:00]
school district can measure. We know if those kids don't hit the market third grade, we lose them
for every year we don't have them at that meets and master's level, at that college-ready level. And
so we are doing great work, and I want to say to the system and say to our board, and say to our
teachers and anybody who else who may be listening, that Spring Branch should be a top choice in a
voucher program. And so, you know, when you go look at that data dashboard, many of the programs
being are the school districts that are highlighted on there are public school districts.

[08:30–09:00]
So colleagues of mine, Katie, Saifair, Klein, they're all at the top, right with us. And so again, I
just want to say when we think about vouchers, school public school districts should be a top option
for parents. And so as that voucher program, bring it on because we're a top choice. So I felt like
it was important to say that. And thank you to all of our teachers and everybody that made that
happen. We also reported recently, this is actually we've had two things that have come out to our
community.

[09:00–09:30]
Several weeks ago, we also reported that Spring Branch ISD is in the 92nd percentile and relative
performance when compared with other districts like us across the state. And let me just tell you,
if you look at the chart, the chart goes from 2013 to 2024. And there have been many, many years
when we were not even in the second uh quartile when we were at the bottom. So this has been a
journey. We have a long ways to go. We um have lots of great gaps to bridge. I am not claiming that
we are there, but we are going to continue to plug away at this and we are going to make this happen
for every kid in our district. So thank you for your support.

[09:30–10:00]
in that. Also, wanted to report that this morning during our senior leadership meeting, we had the
principal of Northbrook High School come and present the RISE a progress report on the RISE Early

well. And in fact, in 18 months, those students will walk across the stage not only with a
springbanch diploma but a two year degree in their hands while on school district time. That is
pretty incredible. Also, want to report that with uh Dr. Hyder was Miss Michelle, the spring
principal.

[10:00–10:30]
at Spring Woods High School, and I'd like to publicly announce that next year we will launch the

materials. We have been working for the better part of a year, really studying that process. We
brought Texas Reads in.

[10:30–11:00]
Courtney and Caroline, you guys have been super helpful to lead that process with us. And if you
remember, last year we did a we had a board report to you all in a board workshop where we uh gave

embed and move forward with structured literacy. And so in their report, um they recommend a process
which actually aligns very closely with what we would do with a textbook adoption, so that was nice.
Um and so just wanted to give

[11:00–11:30]
you a quick report of where we are on that. So the humanities team has looked at all the options.
They did narrow down to two finalists that we are looking at. They are both aligned to the science
of reading. They're comprehensive. So two of the things we really are hearing from our teachers is
number one, our teachers are begging, Courtney, I you know, uh in the your words, are begging for a
comprehensive resource. They don't want a piecemeal resources anymore. They want a comprehensive
program, and that's actually what Texas Reads recommends that we have. So it needs to meet that
standard. And then second of all,

[11:30–12:00]
you know, we have 57 uh or uh 37% of our students who are in a dual language program. So English is
not their first language. So we have to have, and most of those students do speak Spanish, so we
have got to have a comprehensive curriculum that aligns and is in Spanish. So those are two really
important pieces when we look at curriculum. And so where we are with that is that there were
teacher review committees that um met on Monday, November 11th. Each elementary principal was um was
uh nominated two committee members, one teacher from K2, one teacher from 3 5. Um and

[12:00–12:30]
And if they were on a campus with a dual language programming or a bilingual program, they needed to
recommend also a teacher from that program so that they could can view the materials in Spanish. We
also asked for each middle school principal to nominate one committee member. So it was a large
group of teachers that came together. They received on Tuesday, November 12th, they heard from each
of the two finalists vendors about the programs. They presented materials. Each teacher was given a
login credential to share then back with the campus. So each committee member came.

[13:00–13:30]
back with the login and then shared that out with the entire campus. So every teacher right now has
access and has had access to both of those sources so that they can go in, they can look at
everything, they can see everything online, they can ask questions. We are giving each campus a
rubric, has a rubric, and they are required to give feedback on the both materials and aligned with
the rubric, and they have about five weeks to review those materials and provide feedback, and then
we will get feedback from all of the campuses. It's a lengthy process, but again, this is

[13:30–14:00]
no different than what we've always done. It's really nice to have the login that teachers can log
in because in the olden days, I don't want to out any of us that we taught a long time ago, but uh
you know, you had to like go to a central location and go and flip through the books, and that's
kind of how that works. So it's nice that they have the login and they will have that login. I also
wanted to share that the public is invited to view those resources and provide feedback as well. And
so today through the end of this week, um, Friday, November 22nd, at the Technology Training Center,
which is on the west end of our school district, it's at 14330 Memorial Drive.

[14:00–14:30]
30 Memorial Drive. You can come view those materials. There's a QR code you can scan to give

I'm sorry, on Thursday, we extended those hours to 7. So from 8 30 to 7 p.m., people can come in and

grade families last night so that they were aware that they could come view the materials.

[14:30–15:00]
So that's that with literacy materials. I'm almost done, I promise. And then tonight we also have

Spring Branch once again earned a superior rating for the 23rd.

consecutive year. So this district has always managed our finances well and I'm really proud of that

out there again but again last legislative session

Um, they are actually going to hear from Representative Harold Dutton. He'll be with us on Wednesday

The next item on the agenda is public comment on agenda or non-agenda items at board meetings.
Public comment time is set aside for individuals who wish to address the board. During special
meetings, workshops and retreats, comments concerning agenda items, while at regular meetings,
individuals can comment on both agenda and non-agenda items. Individuals who wish to participate
during the

[23:30–24:00]
portion of the meeting designated for public comment shall sign up by completing the appropriate
form on the district website, by submitting the form in person at the ad building, or by sending an
email with the district form as an attachment to the email address listed on the form. Public
comment forms must be received no later than noon on the day of the meeting and shall indicate the
agenda item or topic on which they wish to address the board. The length of public comment shall be
at the discretion of the board president, not to exceed four minutes. We will do two minutes today.
Because individual rights are protected.

[24:00–24:30]
by various laws and board policies. Complaints regarding specific employees or students should be
directed to the administration. If a person makes complaints about a specific employee or student
during public comment, the board president has the discretion to move the board into closed session
to hear such complaints. If anyone makes a complaint about a specific employee by name or by job
title, the board may hold the public comment until the end of the public comments and hear all such
complaints individually in closed session. Under the law, the board cannot deliberate, discuss, or
make a decision on.

[24:30–25:00]
any subject that is not on the agenda. So, as a general matter, the board does not respond to
questions at public comment. Specific factual information or review of existing policy, however,
may, where legally allowed, be provided in response to inquiries or questions. At this time, we will
begin public comment, and our first speaker is Michael Falleck. We ask that you come forward to the
